titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Cynthia Ozick Papers
titlestmt.author: Michael L. Forstrom

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This finding aid, which is compliant with the Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0., has a MARC record in Yale's ILS with the following bib number: 16398046.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Finding aid description and metadata in Archives at Yale are licensed under a CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Thursday September 5, 2024 at 03:14

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.
Boxes 317-318 (videocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 319 (microfilm): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 320 and 326 (optical disks): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 323-324 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

acqinfo.head: Immediate Source of Acquisition
acqinfo.p: Purchased from Melanie Jackson Agency, LLC, on the Sinclair Lewis Fund and the Edwin J. Beinecke Book Fund, 2021.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five series: I. Correspondence, 1931-2021. II. Writings, 1950-2021. III. Printed Materials, 1921-2019. IV. Sound Recordings, 1976-2016. V. Personal and Other Papers, circa 1900-2021.

bioghist.head: Cynthia Ozick (1928-)
bioghist.p: Cynthia Ozick (born April 17, 1928), a fiction writer and essayist, was born in New York City and grew up in the borough of the Bronx. She attended New York University and received an M.A. in literature from Ohio State University. She was married to Bernard Hallote from 1952 until his death in 2017.

Ozick's fictional works often center on Jewish American themes and Jewish history. Her essays comment on politics, history, literature, and writing craft and have appeared in such publications as The New Yorker , The New York review of books , and The New York Times book review . Ozick has won several prominent awards for both fiction and non-fiction, including four first place O. Henry Prizes for short stories; a National Book Critics Circle Award for her essay collection Quarrel & quandary (2000); and a National Humanities Medal. She has been described so often as "the Athena of American letters" that the phrase is of indeterminate coinage. Her better-known works include the short story collection The Pagan rabbi and other stories (1971); the short story "The Shawl" (1980; extended and published as a novella in 1989); the novel The Puttermesser papers (1997); the essay collections Metaphor & memory (1989) and Portrait of the artist as a bad character: and other essays on writing (1996).

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Cynthia Ozick Papers. Yale Collection of American Literature, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library.

scopecontent.head: Content Description
scopecontent.p: The Cynthia Ozick papers consist of correspondence, writings, printed materials, sound recordings, and personal papers that document Ozick's professional and personal activities. The bulk of the papers range in date from Ozick's youth to the library's acquisition of the papers in 2021. Correspondents include Edward Alexander, Saul Bellow, Leon Edel, Irving Feldman, Merrill Joan Gerber, Irving Howe, Susanne Klingenstein, Gordon Lish, Norman Mailer, Oscar Mandel, Amos Oz, Burton Raffel, Abraham Regelson, Philip Roth, Myra Sklarew, John Updike, Helen Weinberg, and Elie Wiesel.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: The Cynthia Ozick Papers is the physical property of the Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University. Literary rights, including copyright, belong to the authors or their legal heirs and assigns. For further information, consult the appropriate curator.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Incoming Correspondence and Outgoing Correspondence.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Upon receipt by the library, storage/shipping boxes containing correspondence only were first rehoused into archival boxes and folders. The contents of a particular storage/shipping box could often be rehoused into a single archival box, though some boxes required rehousing into multiple (from two to four) archival boxes. This was due in part to the fact that most incoming letters were found inside their accompanying envelopes. To facilitate use of the material, letters were removed from their envelopes. As a general practice, envelopes precede the accompanying letter in the arrangement. Correspondence is arranged in chronological order within individual folders only. Boxes have been sorted into a rough chronological arrangement.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Incoming correspondence consists chiefly of original letters to Ozick, but drafts, photocopies, and printouts of letters from Ozick to others, as well as clutches of original letters from Ozick to friends and family, can also be found. Some letters are addressed to both Ozick and her husband Bernard Hallote.

The earliest correspondence appears to be from family and friends. By the early-mid 1960s, correspondence consists chiefly of letters from literary-minded colleagues and friends, and then, as Ozick established herself professionally, her circle and the number of her correspondents grew. Correspondents from the 1960s, for example, include the following: Oscar Mandel, Burton Raffel, Irving Feldman, Florence Victor, and A. Helen Weinberg, as well as her maternal uncle, Abraham Regelson. During the 1970s, letters can be found for individuals and, increasingly, corporate bodies and organizations. Prominent authors, critics and editors from this period include Irving Howe, Philip Roth, Gordon Lish, Tillie Olsen, and Myra Sklarew. Letters from the mid-1980s continue this shift evident already in the 1970s, with some old acquaintances, such as Weinberg and Feldman, more publishers and universities and organizations, including Gordon Lish at Knopf, and generally, a broader, more diverse group. Noteworthy correspondents include: Madeleine Gekiere, Merrill Joan Gerber, Erica Jong, Norma Rosen, Elie Wiesel, and George E. Mutch.
Letters are chiefly in English, with some materials in Hebrew, German, and French. Enclosures include clippings, printed ephemera, photographs, and drafts of writings.

The names of correspondents in select boxes, provided in an inventory that accompanied the Ozick papers, have been listed in folder notes where appropriate. The presence of original letters from Ozick to others is also captured in folder notes.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Incoming correspondence is arranged in chronological order within individual folders only, not continguous or sequential folders within a box. Boxes have been sorted into a rough chronological arrangement based on the earliest inclusive or bulk date in the box date ranges.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Boxes have been sorted into a rough chronological arrangement based on the earliest inclusive or bulk date in the box date ranges.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Outgoing Correspondence subseries consists chiefly of photocopies of letters from Cynthia Ozick to others, though drafts of letters and printouts of letters and email threads can also be found. Incoming correspondence is present as well in scattered folders. These files appear to have been created circa 1970, at which time Ozick or an assistant(s) starting copying her outgoing correspondence, presumably before materials were mailed. It is not known how comprehensive the outgoing correspondence file is, but the practice appears to have been consistent for the period from 1970 until 2010.

did.unittitle: Section of unidentified newspaper removed from Correspondence, circa 1977-1979
Empty envelope removed from Correspondence, circa 2003-2008

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into two subseries: Writings of Cynthia Ozick and Writings of Others.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Series II. consolidates writings by Cynthia Ozick that were grouped together and/or easily identifiable from accompanying summary description. Titles of writings are derived from the text or, in some cases, transcribed from folder headings.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II. consolidates writings by Cynthia Ozick that were grouped together and/or easily identifiable from accompanying summary description. Writings by Ozick include drafts, proofs, and printed versions of work, as well as associated materials such as correspondence, publicity, reviews, and research materials. Material can be found for both shorter works and for novels and collections. For example, various materials can be found for Ozick's novels, The Cannibal galaxy (1983), The Messiah of Stockholm (1987) and Heir to the glimmering world (1996). Similarly, various materials can be found for The Pagan rabbi and other stories (1971) and the essay collections, Art & ardor (1983) and Fame & folly (1996). The presence of multiple drafts, later versions of texts, and associated materials document the evolution of titles, the publication process, Ozick's relationships with editors and publishers, and critical responses to her work.

The largest file in Series II. relates to Ozick's stage adaptation of her short story, "The Shawl". Directed by Sidney Lumet, the play was performed under the title "Blue light" in 1994 at the Bay Street Theatre in Sag Harbor, New York, and in 1996 at the Jewish Repertory Theater "Playhouse 91", New York.
Unidentified and other (short) works by Ozick can be found at the end of the alphabetical run of titles.

The Writings of Others subseries consists of work by others shared with or collected by Ozick. Many works are either inscribed by authors to Ozick or include presentation letters. The subseries includes critical writing on Ozick's work. Here too files of unidentified work, photocopies of work, and reprints can be found at the end of the alphabetical run of authors.
Additional writings by Ozick and others can also be found in Series III. Printed Materials.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Boxes 317-318 (videocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 319 (microfilm): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Arranged alphabetically by title, first line or supplied heading.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four subseries: Clippings, Journals, Newspapers, and Other Materials.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III. consists of clippings, journals, magazines, newspapers, and other materials.

The Clippings file consists of original print clippings from newspapers and journals and other sources as well as photocopies of clippings. Materials are arranged chronologically and housed by size. The content of clippings relates both to Ozick and other matters. Clippings on Ozick specifically include brief mentions and longer, critical pieces.
The Journals and Newspapers subseries are arranged alphabetically by title. Materials include printed versions of work by Ozick and others.
Other materials includes brochures and publicity, catalogs, event programs, postcards, scrapbooks, and other ephemera.
Chiefly in English; some materials in French, Hebrew, Italian, Spanish, and Swedish.

title.part: The American forum of the air

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: Box 323-324 (audiocassettes): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.
Box 326 (audio CDs): Restricted fragile material. Reference copies may be requested. Consult Access Services for further information.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV. consists of sound recordings captured or reproduced on audiocassettes and audio CDs. Recordings ranging in date from the mid-1970s to 2016 include Abraham Regelson reading his work, Cynthia Ozick reading her work, interviews with Ozick, commentary on Ozick, and others reading work by Ozick.

did.unittitle: "AR in N.R., Dec. 76, OPHRA visits New Rochelle, 1/21/77"
"Critics, monsters, fanatics, and other literary essays"
"Cynthia Ozick 5-31-97 [?] 6-4-97 on The Mara Topp Show"
"Cynthia Ozick, 'Envy, or Yiddish in America' [and] 'The Pagan Rabbi'"
"Cynthia Ozick, 'Envy, or Yiddish in America' [and] 'The Pagan Rabbi'" (cases)
"Cynthia Ozick interviewed by Joshua O. Haberman, C.B.S. TV, Washington"
"An Introduction to 'The Shawl' by Cynthia Ozick"
"Mary Catherine Bateson panel, College of New Rochelle, sides III-IV"

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into five subseries: Artwork, Awards, Photographs, Student Papers, and Other Material.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series V. consists of artwork, awards, photographs, student records and other materials.

In addition to sketchbooks with original drawings by Ozick, there are several works of art by others, including a watercolor commemorating Ozick's departure from a job at Filene's in the early 1950s. Ozick received numerous awards for her written work from the 1980s onward, including honorary degrees from academic institutions and literary and other awards from various organizations and institutions. For example, Ozick earned the 2000 Criticism Award from the National Book Critics Circle for Quarrel & quandary , and the Guardian of Zion award for support to the state of Israel, from Bar-Ilan University, in 2001.

Series V. includes photographs of Ozick and others. There are some snapshots of Ozick and family, but many of the photographs of Ozick with others derive from events and award ceremonies, such as commencements with honorary degrees. Photographs of others include family, her husband Bernard Hallote, and her literary agent, Theron Raines. Student materials include notebooks, exam books, report cards and early papers.

The large Other Materials subseries consolidates mixed and miscellaneous files. Files are arranged alphabetically by transcribed and supplied headings. Materials feature publishers files and organizational records compiled later in Ozick's career. Such files include the following: Académie Universelles des Cultures, American Academy of Arts and Letters, the American Academy of Arts and Sciences, Éditions de L'Olivier, Houghton Mifflin, John Simon Guggenheim Memorial Foundation, PEN American Center and Congress, and Rogers, Coleridge & White. These files document Ozick's publishing and professional commitments. There are also address books, contracts, financial records, notebooks, materials relating to the National Book Awards and other events and projects, and publicity and reviews.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: One hundred sixty-two negatives have been removed to be reformatted. Following reformatting, the original negatives, if nitrate, will be destroyed and the reformatted versions will be made available for access.
